text: The surgical procedure is conducted using an anterior transperitoneal approach, with the surgeon accessing the abdominal cavity through the peritoneum.
The surgeon incises the peritoneum at the intersection of the medial umbilical ligament and the vas deferens, using precise surgical technique.
The surgeon dissects the paravesical space, gradually exposing the endopelvic fascia.
The surgeon coagulates and cuts the urachus at the end of the procedure.
The surgeon performs a longitudinal cystostomy, opening the bladder starting at the prostate-vesicle junction.
The surgeon places a stitch in the middle lobe of the bladder to increase traction, facilitating further manipulation of the tissue.
The surgeon identifies the bilateral ureteric orifices to prevent injury during the procedure, ensuring these anatomical structures are clearly visible and preserved.

The surgeon develops the plane between the prostatic adenoma and the pseudocapsule using sharp and blunt dissection, starting at the six o'clock position on the right lobe, followed by developing the plane between the left adenoma lobe and the pseudocapsule.
The surgeon transects the prostatic urethra at the apex, completing the dissection of the prostatic adenoma.
The surgeon retrigonizes the prostatic fossa after achieving hemostasis, using a single layer running suture from the urethra to the bladder neck.
The surgeon performs cystotomy closure using a double-layer running suture technique. A three-way catheter is then inserted, allowing for continuous irrigation.
The surgeon initiates the procedure by performing a posterior midline cystostomy, creating an incision in the bladder to access the surgical site.
The surgeon achieves bladder retraction using three exovulporal sutures, visibly positioning the bladder for further surgical access.
The surgeon identifies the bilateral ureteric orifices to prevent injury during the procedure.
The surgeon incises the bladder neck at the six o'clock position, exposing the prostatic adenoma for further surgical intervention.

The surgeon develops the plane between the prostatic adenoma and the pseudocapsule using a combination of sharp and blunt dissection techniques, carefully separating the adenoma from the surrounding tissue.
The surgeon develops the dissection plane circumferentially around the prostatic adenoma on both the right and left sides, using a combination of sharp and blunt techniques.
The prostatic adenoma is fully dissected, revealing the surrounding anatomical structures.
The surgeon inspects the prostatic fossa for hemostasis and places single stitches to prevent post-operative bleeding.
A three-way catheter is inserted into the prostatic fossa, with continuous irrigation visibly flowing through the catheter.
The surgeon closes the cystostomy incision using a single layer of single stitches, ensuring the incision is securely sealed.
